scenario: A high-throughput worker maintains a long-lived gRPC connection to the Zeebe gateway. The team wonders about connection pooling / reuse in gRPC clients.
question: Does the Zeebe gRPC client pool / reuse connections?

options.text: The Zeebe gRPC client creates a new TCP connection for every individual API call -- this would make high-throughput workers extremely inefficient due to repeated TLS handshakes. gRPC is built on HTTP/2, which multiplexes multiple concurrent requests over a single persistent TCP connection. The Zeebe SDKs create one gRPC Channel at initialisation and reuse it for all subsequent ActivateJobs, CompleteJob, PublishMessage, and other calls throughout the worker's lifetime.

The gRPC connection pool size is configurable per worker to tune throughput -- while some gRPC client implementations expose pool or concurrency configuration, the primary mechanism in Zeebe SDKs is HTTP/2 multiplexing on a single Channel rather than a traditional pool of multiple connections. The Camunda Spring Boot Starter manages the Channel lifecycle automatically. For throughput tuning, adjusting maxJobsActive on the worker is more commonly used than pool configuration.

gRPC connection management is a minor implementation detail irrelevant to Zeebe worker performance -- this underestimates the performance impact. Connection reuse is a major performance factor for high-throughput workers. Avoiding repeated TLS handshakes (which are cryptographically expensive), leveraging HTTP/2 multiplexing for concurrent request handling, and maintaining warm connections for lower latency are all critical for production worker performance at scale.

Yes -- gRPC clients pool connections by design. A single gRPC Channel (Java) or equivalent multiplexes multiple requests over a single TCP connection (HTTP/2). ). Connection reuse is critical for performance -- avoiding new TLS handshakes per call.

a.text: Incorrect. gRPC's underlying transport is HTTP/2, which natively multiplexes multiple streams over a single TCP connection. Zeebe SDKs leverage this: Single Channel (Java) / equivalent abstraction created at SDK initialisation. Multiple operations (ActivateJobs, CompleteJob, PublishMessage, etc.) reuse the same Channel. HTTP/2 multiplexing: multiple concurrent requests share the same TCP connection without head-of-line blocking. Benefits of connection reuse: Avoid TLS handshake overhead per call (handshake is expensive). Lower latency for subsequent calls (warm connection). Reduced resource usage (fewer sockets, fewer file descriptors). Practical implications: Don't create new clients per call -- share the SDK client across handlers within the same application. Camunda Spring Boot Starter (the canonical 8.8 Spring integration -- formerly known as spring-zeebe-sdk, since renamed per apis-tools/camunda-spring-boot-starter/) manages this automatically -- one client per application. Worker scaling: increase maxJobsActive rather than spawning multiple clients. For connection failure handling: SDKs typically auto-reconnect on transient network issues; retry policies handle gRPC-level errors.
